title: Specific heat and susceptibility of S=1/2 antiferromagnets on square, triangular, and kagome lattices
abstract: We study the temperature dependence of the thermodynamic properties of spin-1/2 antiferromagnets on two-dimensional lattices. Our analysis employs the sine-square deformation (SSD), in which a real-space envelope function is applied to the Hamiltonian so that the local energy scale is smoothly reduced to zero at the system boundaries. The quantum eigenstates of the SSD Hamiltonian exhibit bulk-like behavior near the system center, effectively mimicking the thermodynamic limit even in small finite-size calculations. Using these fictitious bulk states, we compute the energy density, specific heat, and magnetic susceptibility as functions of temperature. We find that both the triangular- and kagome-lattice antiferromagnets show either a shoulder or a pronounced double-peak structure in the low-temperature specific heat, whereas the kagome case particularly shows a strong enhancement of magnetic susceptibility down to the lowest temperature range. These direct comparisons, together with the square-lattice and one-dimensional cases, reveal that although both frustrated systems retain a substantial amount of entropy, the low-energy excitations below ~ 0.5J of the kagome lattice are predominantly governed by the magnetic excited states, whereas not much for the triangular lattice.
summary: This paper presents a numerical framework using Sine-Square Deformation to study the thermodynamic properties of frustrated quantum magnets. It successfully captures complex features like double-peak specific heat in kagome lattices, providing a way to mimic the thermodynamic limit using small-scale clusters.
main_problem: The difficulty of accurately calculating the low-temperature thermodynamic properties of frustrated 2D quantum magnets in the thermodynamic limit due to finite-size effects and the sign problem in traditional numerical methods.
main_result: The study identifies a double-peak structure in the specific heat of kagome and triangular lattices and shows that the kagome lattice exhibits a strong enhancement of magnetic susceptibility at low temperatures due to magnetic excited states.
method: The Sine-Square Deformation (SSD) technique is applied to the Hamiltonian to create fictitious bulk states, combined with the Thermal Pure Quantum (TPQ) method for finite-temperature calculations.
model_or_system: S=1/2 antiferromagnetic Heisenberg models on 1D chains, square, triangular, and kagome lattices.
key_observables: Energy density, specific heat (Cv), magnetic susceptibility (chi), and thermal entropy density.
important_parameters: Temperature (T/J), exchange interaction (J), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The SSD method assumes that the center of the deformed system effectively mimics the thermodynamic limit; kernel regression assumes a smooth temperature dependence for extrapolating data.
figures_summary: Figure 1 defines the SSD function and its spatial distribution; Figure 2 compares specific heat and susceptibility across 1D, square, triangular, and kagome lattices; Figure 3 shows the single-particle density of states; Figure 4 demonstrates convergence of energy density; Figure 6 highlights the reduction of the finite-size gap in 1D chains.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SSD method, validates it using free fermion and 1D chain benchmarks, applies it to 2D square lattices to compare with QMC, and finally investigates the thermodynamic properties of frustrated triangular and kagome lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
